Data Summaries Using Existing Columns

Data Summary 1

Question: Which parent companies contribute the most total plastic waste across all countries, and what share of the total recorded waste does each company account for?

Code 
overall_total <- sum(plastics$grand_total, na.rm = TRUE)

company_share <- function(x, overall_total) {
  sum(x, na.rm = TRUE) / overall_total
}

company_summary <- plastics |>
  filter(!is.na(parent_company),
         parent_company != "Grand Total",
         parent_company != "null",
         parent_company != "NULL",
         parent_company != "Unbranded") |>
  group_by(parent_company) |>
  summarize(
    total_plastic_waste = sum(grand_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    share_of_total = company_share(grand_total, overall_total),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(total_plastic_waste)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

company_summary
# A tibble: 10 × 3
   parent_company               total_plastic_waste share_of_total
   <chr>                                      <dbl>          <dbl>
 1 The Coca-Cola Company                      25530        0.0212 
 2 La Doo                                     15221        0.0126 
 3 Universal Robina Corporation                8651        0.00718
 4 Nestle                                      8642        0.00717
 5 Unilever                                    8589        0.00713
 6 Colgate-Palmolive                           6545        0.00543
 7 Barna                                       6225        0.00517
 8 Assorted                                    6101        0.00506
 9 Pure Water, Inc.                            5347        0.00444
10 Pepsico                                     5155        0.00428

Data Summary 2

Question: Which countries had clean up efforts that found the largest grand total of plastics in 2019 and 2020?

Code 
## Uses iteration and a custom function
total_plastics <- function(input_country){
  
  grand_total_plastics <- plastics |>
    filter(country == input_country) |>
    distinct(year, grand_total) |>
    pull(grand_total)
  
  sum(grand_total_plastics, na.rm = TRUE)
}

plastics |>
  distinct(country) |>
  filter(country != "EMPTY") |>
  mutate(
    plastic_total = map_int(country, total_plastics)
    ) |>
  slice_max(plastic_total, n = 10)
# A tibble: 10 × 2
   country                         plastic_total
   <chr>                                   <int>
 1 Taiwan_ Republic of China (ROC)        241292
 2 NIGERIA                                157133
 3 Philippines                            125667
 4 Switzerland                             66588
 5 Nigeria                                 61123
 6 Indonesia                               34173
 7 Vietnam                                 28347
 8 Kenya                                   27426
 9 ECUADOR                                 25346
10 United States of America                21158

Data Summaries That Modify Columns

Data Summary 3

Question: Which countries have the highest average amount of plastic collected per cleanup event?

Code 
plastic_per_event <- function(input_country) {
  plastics |>
    filter(country == input_country) |>
    distinct(year, grand_total, num_events) |>
    summarise(
      total_plastic = sum(grand_total, na.rm = TRUE),
      total_events = sum(num_events, na.rm = TRUE)
    ) |>
    mutate(plastic_per_event = total_plastic / total_events) |>
    pull(plastic_per_event)
}

plastic_event_summary <- plastics |>
  distinct(country) |>
  mutate(plastic_per_event = map_dbl(country, plastic_per_event)) |>
  slice_max(plastic_per_event, n = 10)

plastic_event_summary
# A tibble: 10 × 2
   country                         plastic_per_event
   <chr>                                       <dbl>
 1 Taiwan_ Republic of China (ROC)            30162.
 2 Togo                                        2399.
 3 Bhutan                                      1750 
 4 Benin                                       1066 
 5 ECUADOR                                      818.
 6 Burkina Faso                                 535.
 7 Montenegro                                   356.
 8 Germany                                      304.
 9 Portugal                                     259.
10 Latvia                                       210.

Data Summary 4

Question: Which nations had the highest average participation for cleanup events in 2019 and 2020?

Code 
cleanup_totals <- function(input_country) {
  plastics |>
    filter(country == input_country) |>
    distinct(num_events, volunteers) |>
    mutate(total_vol = sum(volunteers, na.rm = T),
         total_events = sum(num_events, na.rm = T)) |>
    distinct(total_vol, total_events) |>
    mutate(vol_per_event = total_vol / total_events) |>
    pull(vol_per_event)
}

plastics |>
  distinct(country) |>
  mutate(
    avg_participation = map_dbl(country, cleanup_totals),
    rounded_participation = round(avg_participation)
  ) |>
  slice_max(avg_participation, n = 20)

Project Checkpoint #3

Code 
library(glue)
library(httr)
library(tidyjson)

Attaching package: 'tidyjson'
The following object is masked from 'package:stats':

    filter

library(jsonlite)

Attaching package: 'jsonlite'

    read_json
The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':

    flatten
Code 
library(readr)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggalluvial)
library(ggrepel)

Create a function to pull data from the API

Code 
get_gdp <- function(country_code, key = my_key) {
  url <- glue("https://api.api-ninjas.com/v1/gdp?country={country_code};X-Api-Key={key}")
  
  my_data <- safely(fromJSON)(url)
  
  if (is.null(my_data$result)) {
    return(NA)
  } else {
    return(my_data$result |> filter(year %in% c(2019, 2020)))
  }
}

Obtain a list of the top 10 countries in plastic waste totals

Code 
## Create a list of the top 10 countries with the highest total plastics
library(countrycode)

top_plastic_countries <- plastics |>
  distinct(country) |>
  filter(!country %in% c("EMPTY", "Taiwan_ Republic of China (ROC)")) |>  ## Dropping Taiwan because of its contested status in the world (some supplemental data does not include Taiwan)
  mutate(
    plastic_total = map_int(country, total_plastics),
    country_code = countrycode(country, origin = "country.name", destination = "iso3c")
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(country_code)) |> ## Dropping Nigeria's duplicate
  filter(plastic_total != 61123) |> ## Added security for removing the specific duplicate
  slice_max(plastic_total, n = 50) |>
  pull(country_code)

Pull data from the API

Code 
Sys.sleep(20)
## Create a list of the countries with their GDP
gdp_list <- top_plastic_countries |>
  map(get_gdp)

## Double check for NAs
gdp_list |>
  is.na() |>
  sum()

Turn the data into a CSV file

Code 
## Turn this into a tibble to join with our original data frame
gdp_data <- gdp_list |>
  keep(is.data.frame) |>
  bind_rows() |>
  rename(country_code = country)

gdp_data |>
  pull(country_code)

## Saving the csv locally
write.csv(gdp_data, "gdp.csv", row.names = FALSE)
Code 
## Rereading in the data from the local file
gdp_df <- read.csv("gdp.csv")

Join the data sets together

Code 
plastics_top <- plastics |>
  filter(!country %in% c("EMPTY", 
                         "Nigeria", 
                         "Taiwan_ Republic of China (ROC)", 
                         "United Kingdom")) |>
  mutate(
    country_code = countrycode(country, origin = "country.name", destination = "iso3c")
  ) |>
  filter(country_code %in% top_plastic_countries) |>
  left_join(gdp_df)

head(plastics_top)

#   country_code <chr>, gdp_nominal <dbl>, gdp_per_capita_nominal <dbl>,
#   gdp_ppp <dbl>, gdp_per_capita_ppp <dbl>, gdp_ppp_share <dbl>,
#   gdp_growth <dbl>
Code 
renewable_data <- read.csv("renewable-energy.csv")
## Data found from https://github.com/datasets/world-development-indicators/tree/main/indicators/eg.fec.rnew.zs

plastics_top <- plastics_top |>
  left_join(renewable_data |>
              filter(Year %in% c(2019, 2020),
                     Country.Code %in% plastics_top$country_code), 
            by = c("country_code" = "Country.Code", 
                   "year" = "Year")) |>
  select(-Country.Name)

New Data Descriptions

GDP Data Description

The GDP data set has been extracted from the API Ninjas “GDP” API. In this case, the data has been programatically obtained in R by making an API call for each country by its ISO3 country code. Annual Gross Domestic Product (GDP) in U.S. Dollars has been recorded in this data set.

However, for this assignment, the years included have been limited to 2019 and 2020. Therefore, each country will appear twice in this data set corresponding to the two years. The data is in the form of a Tidy Data Frame, and it would later be merged with the plastic data set.

Key variables include:

  • country: ISO3 country code

  • year: year of observation (2019 or 2020)

  • value: GDP (nominal) in U.S. dollars

Renewable Energy Data Description

The renewable energy dataset was sourced from an open-source data set provided on GitHub and is based on World Development Indicators. The data set consists of country-specific information about renewable energy consumption over time.

In this case, the renewable energy data set has been filtered to include only the years 2019 and 2020 as well as only those countries that appear in the plastic data set. This filtering was done using ISO3 codes.

Key variables include:

  • Country Code : ISO3 country code

  • Year: year of observation

  • renewable energy consumption, measured as the percentage of total final energy consumption from renewable sources

This dataset was merged with the GDP and plastics datasets to support comparative analysis across countries.

Additional Data Summaries/Visualizations

RQ: What is the relationship between total plastic was and percentage of renewable energy, for the top 10 countries in total plastic waste ?

Code 
renewable_summary <- plastics_top |>
  filter(country_code %in% top_plastic_countries) |>
  distinct(country, 
           country_code, 
           year, 
           grand_total, 
           Value) |>
  group_by(country, 
           country_code) |>
  summarize(
    total_waste = sum(grand_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    renewable_pct = mean(Value, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(country != "Ecuador") |>
  arrange(desc(total_waste))

renewable_summary
# A tibble: 50 × 4
   country                  country_code total_waste renewable_pct
   <chr>                    <chr>              <dbl>         <dbl>
 1 NIGERIA                  NGA               157133          80.1
 2 Philippines              PHL               125667          27.9
 3 Switzerland              CHE                66588          25.7
 4 Indonesia                IDN                34173          20.7
 5 Vietnam                  VNM                28347          19.8
 6 Kenya                    KEN                27426          68.5
 7 ECUADOR                  ECU                25346          17.7
 8 United States of America USA                21158          10.8
 9 Burkina Faso             BFA                20348          72.8
10 India                    IND                19655          35.1
# ℹ 40 more rows

New Plot: Plastic Waste vs. Renewable Energy Percentage by Country

Code 
ggplot(renewable_summary, aes(x = total_waste, y = renewable_pct)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "black") +
  geom_text_repel(
    aes(label = country_code),
    size = 4,
    box.padding = 0.5,
    point.padding = 0.5
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Plastic Waste vs Renewable Energy by Country",
    subtitle = "Top 50 Countries by Total Plastic Waste",
    x = "Total Plastic Waste",
    y = "Renewable Energy (% of total energy consumption)"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12),
    plot.margin = margin(15, 15, 15, 15),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 12)
  )

New Plot: GDP vs. Total Plastic Waste by Country

Code 
library(ggalluvial)
library(paletteer)
Code 
top10_codes <- plastics_top |>
  distinct(country_code) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  pull(country_code)

country_plot <- plastics_top |>
  filter(country_code %in% top10_codes) |>
  group_by(country, country_code) |>
  summarize(
    total_plastic = sum(grand_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    gdp = mean(gdp_nominal, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(country_plot, aes(x = gdp, y = total_plastic)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = country_code), vjust = -0.5, size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Relationship Between GDP and Total Plastics (Top 10 Countries)",
    x = "GDP (billions USD)",
    y = "Total Plastics"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Project Checkpoint #4

Improved Plot

Code 
renewable_summary <- renewable_summary |>
  mutate(
    group = case_when(
      country_code %in% c("NGA", "CHE", "PHL") ~ country_code,
      total_waste < 40000 & renewable_pct < 40 ~ "Low waste / Low renewable",
      total_waste < 40000 & renewable_pct >= 40 ~ "Low waste / High renewable",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ), 
    group = factor(group, 
      levels = c("Low waste / Low renewable",
                 "Low waste / High renewable",
                 "NGA",
                 "PHL",
                 "CHE")
    )
  )

ggplot(renewable_summary, aes(x = total_waste, y = renewable_pct, color = group)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_text_repel(
    aes(label = country_code),
    size = 2
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Plastic Waste vs Renewable Energy by Country",
    subtitle = "Top 50 Countries by Total Plastic Waste",
    x = "Total Plastic Waste",
    y = "Renewable Energy (% of total energy consumption)",
    color = "Group"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
  "Low waste / Low renewable" = "#1f77b4",  
  "Low waste / High renewable" = "#2ca02c", 
  "Other" = "gray70",
  "NGA" = "#e41a1c", 
  "CHE" = "#ff7f00",  
  "PHL" = "#000000"    
)) +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12),
    plot.margin = margin(15, 15, 15, 15))

RQ: Which countries have the highest amount of plastic waste, and what from what companies does that waste originate from?

Code 
## Get a list of the top 10 companies and the top 5 countries in plastic waste
top10_companies <- unique(company_summary$parent_company)
top5_codes <- top_plastic_countries[1:5]

## Convert the data into the long format
plastics_long <- plastics_top |>
  filter(parent_company %in% top10_companies,
         country_code %in% top5_codes) |>
  select(country, year, parent_company, hdpe, ldpe, o, pet, pp, ps, pvc) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(hdpe, ldpe, o, pet, pp, ps, pvc),
    names_to = "plastic_type",
    values_to = "total_plastic"
  ) |>
  filter(total_plastic > 0)

## Create the Sankey diagram
plastics_long |>
  mutate(
    country = fct_reorder(country, 
                          total_plastic, 
                          sum, 
                          .desc = TRUE),
    parent_company = fct_reorder(parent_company, 
                                 total_plastic, 
                                 sum, 
                                 .desc = TRUE),
    parent_company = recode(parent_company,
    "Universal Robina Corporation" = "Universal Robina",
    "The Coca-Cola Company" = "Coca-Cola")
  ) |> 
  ggplot(aes(axis1 = country,
             axis2 = parent_company,
             y = total_plastic)) +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("Country", "Company"), 
                   expand = c(.2, .05)) +
  geom_alluvium(aes(fill = plastic_type)) +
  geom_stratum() +
  ggfittext::geom_fit_text(stat = "stratum", 
                           aes(label = after_stat(stratum)),  
                           width = 1/4, 
                           min.size = 3) +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_fill_paletteer_d("ggthemes::Color_Blind") +
  xlab("Countries vs. Companies") +
  ylab("Total Plastic") +
  ggtitle("Plastic Waste from Country to Company",
          "Stratified by Plastic Type") + 
  labs(fill = "Plastic Type")

A simple linear regression model was used:

\(Y_i\) = \(\beta_0\) + \(\beta_1 X_i\) + \(\varepsilon_i\)

\(Y_i\) = renewable energy percentage for country \(i\)

\(X_i\) = total plastic waste for country \(i\)

\(\beta_0\) = intercept

\(\beta_1\) = slope parameter

\(\varepsilon_i\sim \text{iid } N(0, \sigma^2)\)

Code 
library(gt)
library(broom)
Code 
## Used the broom package to better format our summary https://broom.tidymodels.org/
model <- lm(renewable_pct ~ total_waste, data = renewable_summary)

tidy(model) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "Linear Regression Results",
    subtitle = "Response: Renewable Energy Percentage | Predictor: Total Waste"
  ) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = list(cells_column_labels(), 
                     cells_title())) |>
  tab_caption("Table 1: Effect of Total Waste on Renewable Energy Percentage") |>
  cols_label(
    term      = "Term",
    estimate  = "Estimate",
    std.error = "Std. Error",
    statistic = "t-Statistic",
    p.value   = "p-Value"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns  = c(estimate, std.error, statistic),
    decimals = 4
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns  = p.value,
    decimals = 4
  )
Table 1: Effect of Total Waste on Renewable Energy Percentage
Linear Regression Results
Response: Renewable Energy Percentage | Predictor: Total Waste
Term Estimate Std. Error t-Statistic p-Value
(Intercept) 28.1728 3.6985 7.6174 0.0000
total_waste 0.0002 0.0001 1.7275 0.0905

A simple linear regression model was conducted to explore the relationship between total plastic waste and renewable energy percentage among the top 10 countries. The estimated slope was 0.0002, showing a slight positive relationship. However, this relationship was not statistically significant from the p value of 0.0905, leading to the conclusion that there is insufficient evidence to conclude that total plastic waste is associated with renewable energy use in these countries.
